inputs.audience: Engineering, R&D, Sales, IT, and anyone who owns a SharePoint folder
inputs.lesson_kind: Foundational. module 2 of the export-controls primer (technical data)

determinations.label: Technical data is a controlled item
determinations.value: Drawings, schematics, PDFs, CAD files, source code, manuals, BOMs, and test results are "technology" under the EAR (or "technical data" under ITAR). Many have ECCNs or USML categories.

determinations.label: Sharing equals exporting
determinations.value: Emailing a controlled PDF abroad, granting cloud access to a foreign person, or letting them view it on a screen all count as a "release." Same posture as a physical shipment.

determinations.label: Nationality matters, location doesn't
determinations.value: Sharing controlled tech with a foreign national inside the U.S. is a deemed export. The recipient's passport, not the meeting room, drives the determination.

determinations.label: Cloud and shared storage count
determinations.value: Putting controlled tech on a server, SharePoint, GitHub, Dropbox, or any system accessible from abroad can be an export the moment a foreign person opens the link.

determinations_confirmed: Technical data is a controlled item

next_actions: Classify technical data before sharing. Even an internal CAD file may need an ECCN or USML category.
Restrict access by nationality on every controlled-tech repo, drive, and Git org.
Log every release: who received it, when, what authority (license, exception, or NLR).
caveats: Some narrow carve-outs exist (e.g., published information under 15 CFR 734.7(a), end-to-end-encrypted-in-transit under 734.18). Don't rely on them without confirming the specific facts.
ITAR technical data has stricter posture. when in doubt, treat ITAR-controlled drawings as if foreign-person access requires DDTC license.
